pattern: Walk a run-length stream lazily: keep a cursor into the (count, value) pairs and, on each request, peel off the asked-for quantity run by run, advancing to the next pair only when the current run empties.
hints: You do not need to expand the encoding into the full sequence; the runs already tell you how many copies of each value remain.
Track which pair you are on and how much of its count is still unused, then a request just subtracts from that remaining count.

For each request, repeatedly take from the current run: if its remaining count covers the request, decrement it and return that value; otherwise consume the whole run, move to the next pair, and continue, returning a sentinel if you run out.

edge_inputs.description: consume across runs and run past the end to get the sentinel
a zero count run is skipped entirely
minimum length encoding, exhaust the single run then ask once more
a single request larger than the whole stream returns the sentinel
exact-fit request lands on the last element of a run
many small requests draining one big run
value zero is a legitimate value, not a sentinel
after exhaustion every later request stays at the sentinel

stress.description: long encoding with many runs and a long mix of small and large requests
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    pairs = max(1, size // 2)
    encoding = []
    for _ in range(pairs):
        encoding.append(rng.randint(0, 8))
        encoding.append(rng.randint(0, 1000))
    ops = ['RLEIterator']
    args = [[encoding]]
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        ops.append('next')
        args.append([rng.randint(1, 12)])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: small counts and requests keep many sentinel and boundary transitions in play
